column2: A phone can make calls, but the user does not want it to use cellular data. What should you check before enabling airplane mode?
column3: Check whether cellular data can be disabled separately while leaving voice service enabled.
column4:
Why

Airplane mode disables cellular networks broadly. Some devices allow a narrower setting that disables mobile data without fully disabling voice service.

Exam Trap

Do not use airplane mode as the first answer when the scenario says voice should remain available.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario Cellular Troubleshooting

column2: A user needs to download a large file on a phone while avoiding cellular data usage. Which network connection should they prefer when available?
column4:
Why

Wi-Fi can provide Internet access without consuming cellular bandwidth, but it depends on local access-point coverage.

Real Help Desk

For large downloads or synchronization jobs, verify the device is actually on Wi-Fi before starting the transfer.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario WiFi Cellular

column2: A user has poor cellular coverage at home but has reliable Wi-Fi. Which feature may allow voice calls to work through the Wi-Fi network?
column3: Wi-Fi calling, if supported by the device and voice carrier.
column4:
Why

Wi-Fi calling can route voice communication through a local Wi-Fi network when cellular coverage is weak or unavailable.

Source Note

The approved source ties Wi-Fi calling to carrier support, so do not assume every phone plan supports it.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Scenario WiFi Voice

column2: A technician moves a removable cellular identity card from one compatible phone to another. What mobile-network component is being moved?
column4:
Why

A physical SIM is a carrier identity component for a cellular device. Moving or replacing it can change the cellular identity associated with the phone.

Exam Trap

Do not confuse a SIM with general storage. Its primary role in this objective is cellular identification and carrier information.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Scenario SIM Cellular

column2: A phone uses a carrier identity that is built into the device and cannot be physically removed. What SIM type is this?
column4:
Why

An eSIM is embedded in the device. It provides SIM functionality without a removable physical card.

Related Cards

1.3-B004 tests recognition of a removable SIM. This card tests the embedded form.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Scenario SIM Cellular

column2: A phone cannot find a new Bluetooth headset during setup. What should you check first on the headset side?
column3: Make sure the headset is in discoverable/pairing mode.
column4:
Why

Bluetooth pairing requires both devices to have Bluetooth enabled, and the accessory may need to be placed into a discoverable mode before it appears.

Exam Trap

Manufacturer-specific button sequences vary. If discoverable mode is not obvious, check the accessory instructions.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario Bluetooth Pairing

column2: A mapping app reports a phone's position. Besides GPS satellites, what other network sources may help mobile location services determine location?
column3: Wi-Fi and cellular tower information may also help determine location.
column4:
Why

Mobile location services can combine GPS, Wi-Fi, and cellular information. GPS is only one input into the broader location-services feature set.

Exam Trap

Do not assume every location result came only from satellite GPS.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.4; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Scenario GPS Location

column2: An organization wants to require screen locks, configure corporate email, and restrict unauthorized apps on employee mobile devices. What type of system should it use?
column4:
Why

MDM centrally applies mobile-device policies, including access controls, app restrictions, and corporate email configuration.

Real Help Desk

When an enterprise phone “sets itself up,” MDM may be applying policy rather than a user manually configuring each setting.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.5; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario MDM Security

column2: An employee owns the phone but uses it for company email and apps. Why is this harder to secure than a fully company-owned device?
column3: It mixes personal ownership/use with company data and requirements, so protecting and removing corporate data is more complicated.
column4:
Why

BYOD devices are both personal and work devices. That creates policy, privacy, and data-protection challenges.

Exam Trap

The ownership model matters. Do not treat BYOD as if the company has the same control it has over a corporate laptop.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.5; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario BYOD Security

column2: A company buys and controls a phone, but allows the employee to use it personally. Which ownership model best matches this?
column4:
Why

COPE means the device is corporate-owned but personally enabled. The organization keeps strong control while permitting personal use.

Related Cards

BYOD starts with employee ownership. CYOD is similar to COPE but gives the user a choice from approved devices.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.5; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Comparison COPE CYOD

column2: A phone is about to synchronize a large amount of business data, and the user has a limited cellular plan. What should you verify before allowing the sync over mobile data?
column3: Verify data restrictions, data caps, and transfer costs, and prefer Wi-Fi when appropriate.
column4:
Why

Synchronization can move enough data to create cost or cap problems. The approved source specifically warns that cellular/mobile and 802.11 choices can have financial consequences.

Real Help Desk

For managed devices, policy may disable cellular downloads or force certain network choices.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.5; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic HighYield Scenario Synchronization Cellular

column2: A user adds a business cloud-storage app to a phone. What two setup tasks are commonly required before data appears on the device?
column3: Authenticate to the service and select which items to synchronize.
column4:
Why

Business applications commonly require username/password authentication and synchronization choices before data appears on the mobile device.

Exam Trap

Installing the app is not always the same as syncing data. Account setup and sync selection may still be required.
column8: Professor Messer 220-1201 v1.70 p.5; CompTIA 220-1201 Exam Objectives v2.0, Objective 1.3
column9: A+::220-1201::1.3 A+::220-1201::MobileDeviceNetworks Basic Scenario Synchronization BusinessApps
